prompt: As a senior elementary school English teacher, I need assistance writing a detailed research paper on a 'New Paradigm of Holistic Empowerment in Elementary English Education and Teaching.' Please provide comprehensive content, suggest relevant keywords, and ensure the paper reflects practical, frontline teaching experience, structured to be helpful for novice teachers.
article:
**Assumptions (stated and used throughout):** “Elementary English” refers to K–6 English education in settings that may be **EFL** (English as a foreign language) or **ESL/MLL** (English as an additional language in multilingual schools). Classrooms may include students with **disabilities/SEN**, mixed proficiency, and culturally/linguistically diverse backgrounds.

---

## Abstract (for your paper)
A “new paradigm of holistic empowerment” in elementary English teaching reframes success from “English scores only” to the **whole child**: language growth *plus* agency, belonging, wellbeing, identity affirmation, and equitable access. This paper synthesizes major foundations—whole-child/WSCC, SEL, self-determination theory (SDT), culturally sustaining pedagogy (CSP), Universal Design for Learning (UDL 3.0), multiliteracies, and translanguaging—and translates them into **frontline, novice-teacher-ready classroom structures**: predictable routines, autonomy-supportive interaction, dialogic talk moves, cooperative learning roles, guided play, task-based lessons, scaffolds for multilingual learners, and inclusive assessment practices. It also outlines assessment systems that evidence holistic outcomes without narrowing learning, and it addresses 2026 implementation realities: AI/digital literacies, online safety, privacy, screen-time balance, equity gaps, and family partnership.

---

## 1) Why a “Holistic Empowerment” Paradigm Now (Problem + Opportunity)

### 1.1 The mismatch novice teachers feel on the ground
New elementary English teachers often experience three daily tensions:

1. **“Cover content” vs. “build language”:** Students need meaning, confidence, and repeated practice—not just completing pages.
2. **“Manage behavior” vs. “build relationships”:** Control-based management can silence language risk-taking; relationship-based structures increase participation.
3. **“Raise scores” vs. “teach the child”:** Children’s emotional safety, identity, and peer belonging can determine whether they speak at all.

A holistic empowerment paradigm treats those tensions not as teacher failure but as a **design challenge**: build conditions where children *want* to participate, *can* participate, and *feel safe* participating.

### 1.2 Whole-child as the umbrella
ASCD’s Whole Child approach explicitly shifts education away from narrowly defined academic achievement toward long-term development and success of all children and emphasizes sustainable, collaborative action across stakeholders [[1]](https://www.ascd.org/whole-child). The CDC’s WSCC (Whole School, Whole Community, Whole Child) model similarly frames child development as cognitive, physical, social, and emotional—and calls for alignment between education and health systems, plus family/community partnership [[2]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_cdc_gov/healthyschools/wscc/index.htm).  

**Implication for elementary English:** English class is not only “language input.” It is a daily place to build **belonging, identity safety, and agency** that make language learning possible.

---

## 2) A Definition You Can Use in the Paper (Operational, Teacher-Friendly)

### 2.1 Working definition
**Holistic empowerment in elementary English education** is a classroom and school approach that intentionally develops:

- **Language competence** (listening, speaking, reading, writing, viewing, presenting)
- **Learner agency** (voice, choice, self-regulation, goal-setting, reflection)
- **Belonging and wellbeing** (emotional safety, peer connection, joyful engagement)
- **Identity and equity** (cultural/linguistic affirmation; anti-bias access; inclusion for MLLs and SEN)
- **Future-ready literacies** (multimodal, digital, media/AI literacy)

…through **evidence-informed design** (UDL/SEL/SDT) and **frontline routines** that make empowerment visible in daily lessons.

### 2.2 A practical “theory of change” (simple)
If teachers design English learning that:
1) supports **autonomy, competence, relatedness** (SDT),  
2) removes barriers and builds **learner agency** (UDL 3.0),  
3) affirms culture/language and sustains pluralism (CSP + translanguaging), and  
4) explicitly teaches social-emotional skills and builds a safe community (SEL + whole-child),  

then students will show:
- more participation and willingness to speak,
- stronger sustained motivation,
- better language growth over time,
- improved equity of outcomes (especially for marginalized learners).

---

## 3) Theoretical Foundations (What to cite, and how it connects to classroom moves)

### 3.1 SEL (social and emotional learning) as “participation infrastructure”
CASEL frames SEL as building skills and environments that advance learning and development and emphasizes equitable learning environments, culturally responsive practice, and student agency/partnership [[3]](https://casel.org/fundamentals-of-sel/what-is-the-casel-framework/). CASEL also highlights “SAFE” implementation (Sequenced, Active, Focused, Explicit) as a marker of effective SEL instruction [[3]](https://casel.org/fundamentals-of-sel/what-is-the-casel-framework/).  

**Classroom translation:** Teach discussion skills (agree/disagree respectfully), self-management routines (calm-down strategy + re-entry), and reflection language (“I used… I will try…”). SEL isn’t “extra”—it is how you get more kids speaking more often.

**Evidence note:** A 2011–2021 meta-analysis of SEL programs for elementary/middle school found SEL is associated with gains across domains, but also notes academic achievement effects can be inconsistent by subject area and cites examples where English language arts gains were not significant in some cases [[37]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12649258/).  
**So:** SEL supports empowerment strongly, but you still must design language-learning practice deliberately.

---

### 3.2 SDT (Self-Determination Theory): empowerment mechanism for motivation + lower anxiety
SDT argues learners thrive when three psychological needs are supported: **autonomy, competence, relatedness** [[6]](https://stial.ie/resources/Ryan%20and%20Deci%202020%20self%20determination%20theory.pdf). A 2025 meta-analysis on SDT and language achievement synthesized 21 primary studies (1999–2024; N≈24,470) and reported that **autonomous motivation** correlates positively with language achievement (overall autonomous motivation r≈0.23) while controlled motivation was not significant; autonomous motivation was also negatively associated with anxiety (≈ −0.23) and controlled motivation positively associated with anxiety (≈ +0.16) [[7]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/2025_AlamerRobatEtAl_L2.pdf).  

**Classroom translation:**
- Autonomy: bounded choices (topic, partner, output mode)
- Competence: scaffolded success + action-oriented feedback
- Relatedness: cooperative structures, predictable kindness, peer support norms

**Frontline reminder:** autonomy support is not “letting kids do anything.” It is *structured choice + rationale + respectful listening*.

A detailed autonomy-supportive teaching framework distinguishes autonomy-supportive vs controlling teaching and lists observable behaviors (listening time, asking student preferences, allowing students to work their way, providing rationales, informational feedback rather than controlling praise) and links autonomy support to engagement, creativity, intrinsic motivation, wellbeing, conceptual understanding, and achievement [[63]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2006_ReeveJang_JEP.pdf).

---

### 3.3 UDL 3.0: design for agency, identity, joy, and access (not “accommodations later”)
CAST’s UDL Guidelines 3.0 (released July 30, 2024) explicitly set **learner agency** as the goal [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/). UDL 3.0 highlights, among others:
- **Optimize choice and autonomy** and **nurture joy and play** [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/)
- **Foster belonging and community** and **collaboration/collective learning** [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/)
- **Cultivate respect across languages and dialects** and **address bias in language/symbols** [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/)
- **Multiple media for communication** and graduated supports for fluency [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/)

UDL 3.0 also responds to calls to address barriers rooted in bias and systems of exclusion [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/).  

**Classroom translation:** Offer multiple ways to access a story (read-aloud, pictures, gestures, home-language preview) and multiple ways to show learning (oral retell, comic strip, audio recording), while teaching the same high-level goal.

---

### 3.4 Culturally Sustaining Pedagogy (CSP): identity and pluralism as learning outcomes
CSP is a strengths-based approach that centers and sustains cultural and linguistic identities and treats culture as dynamic; it aims for pride, belonging, and meaningful pathways [[14]](https://ies.ed.gov/rel-pacific/2025/01/culturally-sustaining-pedagogy-infographic). A CSP framework emphasizes centering community languages/knowledge, community agency, structured opportunities to contend with internalized oppressions, and systemwide support [[14]](https://ies.ed.gov/rel-pacific/2025/01/culturally-sustaining-pedagogy-infographic). The CSP chapter framing also argues for additive schooling—“remaining whole,” critically enriching strengths rather than replacing them [[15]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/oa-edit/10.4324/9780203774977-18/culturally-sustaining-pedagogy-samy-alim-django-paris-casey-philip-wong).  

**Classroom translation:** Don’t just “include multicultural holidays.” Build routines where students’ languages and stories are legitimate resources for meaning-making, and teach English as an *additional tool*, not as a replacement identity.

---

### 3.5 Translanguaging: practical equity tool (with real constraints)
A 2022 systematic review of pedagogical translanguaging in TESOL (20 classroom-based empirical studies) found translanguaging often supports inclusivity, participation, and English learning, but also faces logistical constraints and strong language-separation ideologies [[33]](https://tesl-ej.org/wordpress/issues/volume26/ej103/ej103a4/). It noted ESL contexts emphasized equity more; EFL contexts often used translanguaging for reinforcement and rapport [[33]](https://tesl-ej.org/wordpress/issues/volume26/ej103/ej103a4/).  

WIDA’s 2025 classroom translanguaging guide frames translanguaging as a mindset that values linguistic assets, improves comprehension, affirms identities/belonging, and supports critical thinking through cross-language comparison; it recommends multilingual resources and welcoming language environments [[48]](https://wida.wisc.edu/news/guide-translanguaging-classroom). WIDA’s 2020 ELD framework explicitly includes translanguaging and an asset-based belief system that links language development with social-emotional and cognitive development [[111]](https://wida.wisc.edu/sites/default/files/resource/WIDA-ELD-Standards-Framework-2020-Edition-Grades-6-8.pdf).  

**Classroom translation:** Strategic L1/home-language use can be a scaffold (preview, vocabulary bridges, idea planning) while still protecting English practice time.

**Debate caution:** A 2025 systematic review on translanguaging across settings stresses that translanguaging is not “universally empowering” in all contexts and emphasizes policy/institutional constraints and conceptual debates [[18]](https://research-portal.uu.nl/ws/files/264540952/Review_of_Education_-_2025_-_Moraru_-_Translanguaging_within_and_across_learning_settings_A_systematic_review_focused_on.pdf).  
**So:** use translanguaging intentionally (for access + identity), not as an unstructured free-for-all.

---

### 3.6 Multiliteracies: English as multimodal meaning-making (not only print)
Research on multiliteracies in primary classrooms highlights multimodality and learner diversity as common emphases in studies (review of 67 studies) [[26]](https://journals.library.ualberta.ca/langandlit/index.php/langandlit/article/view/29333), grounded historically in challenging monolingual, narrow definitions of literacy [[27]](https://licsjournal.org/index.php/LiCS/article/view/3127/3130).  

**Classroom translation:** In K–6, “text” includes images, diagrams, gestures, audio, video, and digital compositions—especially important for multilingual learners and younger children.

---

### 3.7 Cooperative learning + dialogic teaching: empowerment through talk distribution
Cooperative learning research traditions emphasize structuring five elements: positive interdependence, accountability, promotive interaction, social skills, group processing [[50]](http://www.co-operation.org/what-is-cooperative-learning). Dialogic teaching emphasizes classroom talk that is collective, reciprocal, supportive, cumulative, purposeful—and provides concrete teacher “talk moves” to extend thinking (say more, revoice, evidence, counterexample) [[51]](https://robinalexander.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/RPIE-2018-Alexander-dialogic-teaching.pdf).  

**Classroom translation:** If you want “student voice,” you must engineer it. Otherwise, the same confident few speak.

---

## 4) What Recent Research Says About Outcomes (2020–Feb 2026) — and Limits

### 4.1 Motivation, agency, and anxiety (strong support)
- SDT-language meta-analysis shows autonomous motivation is positively related to language achievement and negatively related to anxiety [[7]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/2025_AlamerRobatEtAl_L2.pdf).  
**Practical meaning:** empowerment practices that increase autonomy and relatedness are not “soft”; they are plausibly linked to achievement and lower anxiety.

### 4.2 SEL outcomes (broad benefits; academic links vary)
- SEL meta-analysis (elementary/middle) explicitly acknowledges inconsistent academic achievement effects by subject area and notes examples where ELA gains were not significant in some studies [[37]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12649258/).  
**Practical meaning:** SEL should be embedded into English tasks (discussion, reflection, peer feedback) rather than treated as a separate program that you hope will raise reading scores by itself.

### 4.3 Translanguaging outcomes (often positive, but context-bound)
- Translanguaging systematic review in TESOL reports benefits (inclusion, participation, facilitating English learning) and challenges (ideology of separation, logistics) [[33]](https://tesl-ej.org/wordpress/issues/volume26/ej103/ej103a4/).  
**Practical meaning:** successful translanguaging requires clear norms, administrator understanding, and intentional planning—not just teacher permission.

### 4.4 Integrated bilingual/CLIL models (positive trends + assessment validity concerns)
A 2024 systematic review of CLIL in “languages other than English” found many studies report positive or mixed linguistic outcomes and highlights context variability; it also reports teacher concerns that standardized tests in the dominant language can yield skewed results for immersion learners, risking harmful decisions [[40]](https://public-pages-files-2025.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2024.1447270/pdf).  
**Practical meaning for English class:** don’t over-trust monolingual, one-shot tests as the only indicator of learning—use multiple evidence sources.

### 4.5 Technology and AI supports (promise + affective limits + governance needs)
A 2025 systematic review of AI chatbots in L2 education (30 studies, 2020–2024) reports improvements especially in speaking and writing, with mechanisms including increased practice opportunities and anxiety reduction, but also notes limits in emotional nuance and deep contextual interaction [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2215039025000086).  
**Practical meaning:** AI can support practice; it cannot replace belonging, relational safety, and teacher judgment.

---

## 5) The New Paradigm in Practice: Core Principles (Novice-Friendly)

Below is a set of **usable principles** you can teach novice teachers. Each principle includes “what it looks like Monday morning.”

### Principle A — “Belonging first, then output”
- **Why:** Relatedness supports motivation (SDT) [[6]](https://stial.ie/resources/Ryan%20and%20Deci%202020%20self%20determination%20theory.pdf) and CASEL emphasizes safe, caring relationships and identity support [[3]](https://casel.org/fundamentals-of-sel/what-is-the-casel-framework/).  
- **Looks like:** predictable greetings, partner routines, no public shaming for errors, structured kindness norms, calm-down/re-entry plan (trauma-informed) [[64]](https://wasa-oly.org/WASA/images/WASA/6.0%20Resources/Hanover/BEST%20PRACTICES%20FOR%20TRAUMA-INFORMED%20INSTRUCTION%20.pdf).

### Principle B — “Agency by design (not by personality)”
- **Why:** UDL goal is learner agency [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/); autonomy-supportive teaching increases engagement and wellbeing [[63]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2006_ReeveJang_JEP.pdf).  
- **Looks like:** bounded choices (choose 1 of 3 prompts), student-generated examples, co-created success criteria, reflection language stems.

### Principle C — “Scaffold up to grade-level meaning”
- **Why:** WIDA frames equity as high expectations with scaffolding up and multimodality [[111]](https://wida.wisc.edu/sites/default/files/resource/WIDA-ELD-Standards-Framework-2020-Edition-Grades-6-8.pdf); WWC practice guide emphasizes integrating oral/written language into content and structured writing opportunities [[70]](https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/Docs/practiceguide/english_learners_pg_040114.pdf).  
- **Looks like:** visuals, gestures, sentence frames, cooperative rehearsal, and multiple retell attempts—not watered-down thinking.

### Principle D — “Language is cultural; English class sustains identities”
- **Why:** CSP sustains linguistic/cultural pluralism [[14]](https://ies.ed.gov/rel-pacific/2025/01/culturally-sustaining-pedagogy-infographic), [[15]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/oa-edit/10.4324/9780203774977-18/culturally-sustaining-pedagogy-samy-alim-django-paris-casey-philip-wong).  
- **Looks like:** multilingual word walls, home-language resources, identity-safe texts (“mirrors/windows”), and respectful stance toward dialects/languages (UDL 3.0) [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/).

### Principle E — “Joy and play are not a break from learning”
- **Why:** UDL explicitly includes joy and play [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/); IES describes guided play supporting language development and academic outcomes and notes guided play can outperform direct instruction for academic content for children under eight [[52]](https://ies.ed.gov/learn/blog/prioritizing-play-importance-play-based-learning-early-education).  
- **Looks like:** guided role-play, language games with clear targets, playful rehearsal, short direct instruction embedded into play.

---

## 6) Frontline Classroom Structures (Daily Routines That Operationalize Empowerment)

This section is the “how-to core” for novice teachers.

### 6.1 Predictable routines and procedures (empowerment through safety + time)
The IRIS Center emphasizes that procedures must be simple, explicitly taught, practiced, reviewed, and reinforced; it provides a practical sequence: introduce, discuss why, model, practice, review, and use behavior-specific praise [[45]](https://iris.peabody.vanderbilt.edu/module/beh2_elem/cresource/q2/p05/).  

**Empowerment logic:** predictable routines reduce cognitive load and anxiety; students spend more time using English and less time guessing teacher expectations.

**Novice routine set (starter pack):**
1. **Entrance routine (2 minutes):** greeting + “Today I feel…” choice card + sit with materials ready.
2. **Warm-up (5 minutes):** choral response / quick talk pairs.
3. **Input (8–10 minutes):** story/pictures with gestures.
4. **Guided practice (10 minutes):** sentence frame talk + teacher circulates.
5. **Production (10–15 minutes):** cooperative task/product.
6. **Reflection (3 minutes):** “I can…” + exit ticket.

---

### 6.2 Autonomy-supportive teacher language (high impact, low cost)
Use autonomy-supportive behaviors identified in SDT teaching research:
- ask what students want/prefer,
- provide rationales,
- allow students to work in their own way,
- give informational feedback rather than controlling praise,
- listen more and use perspective-taking statements [[63]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2006_ReeveJang_JEP.pdf).

**Concrete “teacher talk” swaps:**
- Instead of: “Stop talking.” → “We need quiet so everyone can hear. After I explain, you’ll choose a partner to practice.”
- Instead of: “Do it like this.” → “Try one of these two ways. If you get stuck, I’ll give a hint.”
- Instead of: “Good job!” → “You used the past tense correctly—your listener can understand the time.”

---

### 6.3 Cooperative learning structures (equity of voice)
Cooperative learning requires explicit structuring of interdependence, accountability, and social skills [[50]](http://www.co-operation.org/what-is-cooperative-learning).  

**Novice-friendly roles (K–6):**
- Speaker 1 / Speaker 2 (rotating)
- Encourager (“Good idea. Add on?”)
- Reporter (shares to class)
- Materials manager

**Group processing (2 minutes):** “What helped us speak English today? What will we try next time?” (directly from cooperative learning emphasis on group processing) [[50]](http://www.co-operation.org/what-is-cooperative-learning).

---

### 6.4 Dialogic teaching talk moves (teach kids how to think aloud)
Dialogic teaching principles: collective, reciprocal, supportive, cumulative, purposeful [[51]](https://robinalexander.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/RPIE-2018-Alexander-dialogic-teaching.pdf).  

**Novice “talk-move” cheat sheet (printable):**
- “Say more.”
- “Who can rephrase that?”
- “What’s your evidence?”
- “Do you agree or disagree? Why?”
- “Can someone add on?”
- “What’s a different idea?”

These moves distribute talk and reduce the “teacher-IRE trap.”

---

### 6.5 Guided play for younger grades (K–2, and still useful in 3–4)
IES distinguishes free play vs guided play and describes guided play types (inquiry play, collaboratively designed play, playful learning, learning through games) [[52]](https://ies.ed.gov/learn/blog/prioritizing-play-importance-play-based-learning-early-education). NAEYC similarly emphasizes guided play keeps child agency while adding intentional learning goals and notes guided play can support vocabulary learning more than free play [[53]](https://www.naeyc.org/resources/pubs/yc/summer2022/power-playful-learning).  

**Example: “Toy Store English” (Grade 1–2)**
- Target: “I want… / How much…? / Here you are.”
- Setup: price tags + picture cards
- Teacher role: guided play—model once, then circulate with prompts, not constant correction.

---

### 6.6 Task-based language teaching for beginners (young learners can do tasks)
Ellis argues TBLT can work for beginner young learners through **input-based tasks**, with design features including topic choice, non-verbal task devices, planned target language, input modification, and allowing L1 for clarification [[62]](https://www.jbe-platform.com/content/journals/10.1075/ltyl.19005.ell).  

**Novice input-based task pattern (Grades 2–6):**
1. **Listen and do:** students follow picture-based instructions.
2. **Match and order:** sequence story cards.
3. **Spot the difference:** information gap with visuals.
4. **Retell with support:** sentence frames + gestures.
5. **Repeat task (short):** second attempt with improved fluency.

---

### 6.7 Sentence frames as equity tools (especially for MLLs and shy speakers)
Colorín Colorado defines sentence frames/starters as structures that support academic language in speaking/writing; it recommends differentiating frames by proficiency, modeling, practicing, posting, celebrating use, and gradually removing scaffolds [[59]](https://www.colorincolorado.org/teaching-ells/ell-classroom-strategy-library/sentence-frames).  

**Frontline tip:** sentence frames increase participation immediately, but only if you:
- keep them **open** (not one correct answer),
- let students choose to use them or try their own,
- use them for **discussion moves** (agree/disagree/add on) [[59]](https://www.colorincolorado.org/teaching-ells/ell-classroom-strategy-library/sentence-frames).

---

### 6.8 A research-based lesson delivery frame for MLLs: SIOP (as a planning checklist)
SIOP is presented as a research-based model with 8 components (lesson prep, building background, comprehensible input, strategies, interaction, practice/application, lesson delivery, review/assessment) [[46]](https://sites.google.com/erlc.ca/ell/archived-pages/programming/sheltered-instruction). A SIOP handout also lists features like posted content & language objectives, key vocabulary emphasis, frequent interaction, wait time, and L1 for clarification [[71]](https://static.sched.com/hosted_files/siopnationalconference2019/73/8%20components%20with%20techniques.pdf).  

**Use it as a “don’t forget” planning tool** for novice teachers.

---

## 7) A “New Paradigm” Lesson Framework (One You Can Teach Novices)

### The H.E.A.R.T. lesson cycle (Holistic Empowerment in Action, Routines, and Teaching)
This is not a published model; it is a practical synthesis **aligned to** the cited frameworks (UDL/SEL/SDT/SIOP/WIDA). You can include it in your paper as a proposed framework.

1) **H — Human connection (2–4 min)**  
Greeting + quick emotion check + success reminder (CASEL climate; trauma-informed safety) [[3]](https://casel.org/fundamentals-of-sel/what-is-the-casel-framework/), [[64]](https://wasa-oly.org/WASA/images/WASA/6.0%20Resources/Hanover/BEST%20PRACTICES%20FOR%20TRAUMA-INFORMED%20INSTRUCTION%20.pdf).

2) **E — Engage with meaning (8–10 min)**  
Story/pictures/real objects + joy/play + identity relevance (UDL: joy/play; relevance/authenticity) [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/).

3) **A — Access with scaffolds (8–12 min)**  
Vocabulary + gestures + frames + multilingual bridges as needed (WIDA multimodality & translanguaging; UDL representation) [[111]](https://wida.wisc.edu/sites/default/files/resource/WIDA-ELD-Standards-Framework-2020-Edition-Grades-6-8.pdf), [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/), [[48]](https://wida.wisc.edu/news/guide-translanguaging-classroom).

4) **R — Rehearse in community (10–15 min)**  
Cooperative roles + dialogic talk moves + structured turn-taking (cooperative essentials; dialogic principles) [[50]](http://www.co-operation.org/what-is-cooperative-learning), [[51]](https://robinalexander.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/RPIE-2018-Alexander-dialogic-teaching.pdf).

5) **T — Transfer & reflect (3–6 min)**  
Small performance (retell, role-play, mini-write) + self-assessment (“I can…”) (formative assessment; ELP goal-setting logic) [[117]](https://www.teachingenglish.org.uk/professional-development/teachers/teaching-knowledge-database/d-h/formative-assessment), [[124]](https://rm.coe.int/16804932bd).

---

## 8) Assessment Without Narrowing Learning (Evidence of Empowerment + Language)

### 8.1 What counts as evidence in this paradigm
You still assess language, but you also collect evidence of:
- participation growth (risk-taking, volume of talk),
- strategy use (frames, clarification questions),
- collaboration and reflection,
- multimodal meaning-making,
- progress over time (not just one-shot performance).

### 8.2 Formative assessment as daily steering, not end-of-unit judgment
British Council defines formative assessment as information for learners and teachers about how well something has been learned so they decide next steps; it includes peer formative feedback examples (students creating tasks for each other and giving feedback) [[117]](https://www.teachingenglish.org.uk/professional-development/teachers/teaching-knowledge-database/d-h/formative-assessment). British Council program guidance also notes peer/self-assessment encourages responsibility and active learning roles [[118]](https://www.britishcouncil.org.ua/en/english/children/parent-centre/yl-course-structure/primary-plus/assessment).  

**Novice “3-minute formative checks”:**
- thumbs signals + quick why,
- one-sentence exit ticket,
- partner retell with a checklist,
- teacher “listen-and-note” during pair talk (2 target features).

### 8.3 WIDA tools to link instruction, differentiation, and family communication
WIDA clarifies assessment purposes (identification, placement, progress, achievement) [[109]](https://wida.wisc.edu/assess). WIDA Can Do Descriptors support differentiating instruction and classroom assessment and interpreting scores [[110]](https://wida.wisc.edu/teach/can-do/descriptors). WIDA also provides score report types and emphasizes translated Individual Student Reports for family conferences [[112]](https://wida.wisc.edu/assess/access/scores-reports).  

**Practical use for novices:**
- Use Can Do descriptors to set **reachable speaking targets** for each proficiency band.
- Use ACCESS domain composites (oral language, literacy, comprehension, overall) to avoid “one score explains everything” thinking [[112]](https://wida.wisc.edu/assess/access/scores-reports).
- Bring translated reports to conferences and pair them with classroom work samples.

### 8.4 Accessibility and accommodations (equity guardrails)
WIDA outlines layered accessibility supports: universal design base, administrative considerations, universal tools, and accommodations for students with IEP/504—while stating modifications that change what the test measures are not allowed [[113]](https://wida.wisc.edu/assess/accessibility-accommodations).  

**Novice move:** Build UDL supports into instruction first (multiple modes), then use accommodations as needed—so students aren’t “surprised” by the assessment format.

### 8.5 CEFR Companion Volume: broaden outcomes, avoid gatekeeping
The CEFR Companion Volume (2020) explicitly states it was never intended to justify gate-keeping assessment [[123]](https://rm.coe.int/common-european-framework-of-reference-for-languages-learning-teaching/16809ea0d4) and expands descriptors for mediation, plurilingual/pluricultural competence, and online interaction [[123]](https://rm.coe.int/common-european-framework-of-reference-for-languages-learning-teaching/16809ea0d4).  

**How to use in elementary English:** treat CEFR-style “can do” statements as **growth descriptors**, not barriers. Use mediation (e.g., “explain a picture to a friend,” “help a classmate understand directions”) as legitimate language goals.

### 8.6 Self-assessment and portfolios for young learners (with teacher mediation)
The Council of Europe’s ELP guidance emphasizes young learners need goal-setting and “I can” checklists; self-assessment requires teacher mediation and cannot rely on a grid alone, and contexts vary so generic lists don’t fit all classrooms [[124]](https://rm.coe.int/16804932bd).  

**Portfolio that fits real classrooms (simple):**
- 1 speaking audio (beginning of term + end),
- 1 illustrated writing sample,
- 1 “I can” checklist with student reflection,
- 1 peer-feedback note (“You did well because…”),
- teacher comment: “Next step.”

### 8.7 Testing standards and fairness (especially with technology)
The Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing are positioned by NCME as global “gold standard” guidance; the 2014 revision emphasizes accountability, accessibility/fairness, and technology issues such as automated scoring and proprietary algorithms [[125]](https://ncme.org/resources/books/testing-standards/).  

**Implication for 2026:** when schools use digital tools (including AI feedback), teachers must preserve:
- transparency of criteria,
- fairness for multilingual learners and students with disabilities,
- human override and professional judgment.

---

## 9) Teacher Competencies and Professional Learning Pathways (What novices must build)

### 9.1 Core competency set for holistic empowerment
**(1) Autonomy-supportive pedagogy** (teacher language + lesson design) [[63]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2006_ReeveJang_JEP.pdf)  
**(2) Inclusive design (UDL 3.0) for agency, identity, access** [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/)  
**(3) Multilingual pedagogy** (WIDA integration of content and language; translanguaging as scaffold) [[111]](https://wida.wisc.edu/sites/default/files/resource/WIDA-ELD-Standards-Framework-2020-Edition-Grades-6-8.pdf), [[48]](https://wida.wisc.edu/news/guide-translanguaging-classroom)  
**(4) Classroom management that maximizes engagement** (structure, expectations, high opportunities to respond) [[72]](https://dropoutprevention.org/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/Simonsen_Fairbanks_Briesch_Myers_Sugai_2008.pdf)  
**(5) Trauma-informed, safety-centered routines** [[64]](https://wasa-oly.org/WASA/images/WASA/6.0%20Resources/Hanover/BEST%20PRACTICES%20FOR%20TRAUMA-INFORMED%20INSTRUCTION%20.pdf)  
**(6) Assessment literacy** (formative, rubric feedback, accommodations, multiple evidence sources) [[117]](https://www.teachingenglish.org.uk/professional-development/teachers/teaching-knowledge-database/d-h/formative-assessment), [[113]](https://wida.wisc.edu/assess/accessibility-accommodations)  
**(7) Reflective practice** (structured cycles) [[76]](https://libguides.cam.ac.uk/reflectivepracticetoolkit/models)

### 9.2 Classroom management: empowerment needs structure, not looseness
Evidence-based classroom management synthesis identifies five critical features: maximize structure; teach/review/reinforce expectations; actively engage students; acknowledge appropriate behavior; respond to inappropriate behavior with a continuum of strategies [[72]](https://dropoutprevention.org/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/Simonsen_Fairbanks_Briesch_Myers_Sugai_2008.pdf).  

**English-class specific “engagement” move:** increase **opportunities to respond** (choral response, response cards, quick partner rehearsals) to raise language practice density [[72]](https://dropoutprevention.org/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/Simonsen_Fairbanks_Briesch_Myers_Sugai_2008.pdf).

### 9.3 Trauma-informed practices: safety is a language intervention
Trauma-informed guidance notes trauma can present as fight/flight/freeze and can mimic disabilities; it emphasizes predictable routines, calm spaces, explicit norms, and resisting re-traumatization [[64]](https://wasa-oly.org/WASA/images/WASA/6.0%20Resources/Hanover/BEST%20PRACTICES%20FOR%20TRAUMA-INFORMED%20INSTRUCTION%20.pdf). NCTSN trauma-informed school practices include threshold greetings and calm-down corners with explicit teaching of use [[66]](https://www.nctsn.org/sites/default/files/resources/brief/supporting-trauma-informed-schools-to-keep-students-in-the-classroom-brief-1.pdf).  

**Frontline reality:** a child who does not feel safe will not speak. Trauma-informed routines protect participation.

### 9.4 Professional learning that actually changes teaching
- PLC research review identifies effective PLC characteristics (shared norms, focus on student learning, reflective dialogue, deprivatizing practice, collaboration) and cautions that “PLC” can become an empty label without these features [[77]](https://www.psycholosphere.com/A%20review%20on%20research%20on%20the%20impact%20of%20PLCs%20on%20teaching%20practice%20&%20student%20learning%20by%20Vescio,%20Ross%20&%20Adams.pdf).  
- A meta-analysis of 60 causal studies of teacher coaching reports positive effects on teacher instruction (≈0.49 SD) and student achievement (≈0.18 SD), with larger effects when coaching is paired with group training [[79]](https://www.matthewakraft.com/s/Kraft-Blazar-Hogan-2018-Teacher-Coaching-RER.pdf).  

**Recommendation for novice teachers:** Pair weekly collaborative planning (PLC) with short coaching cycles (plan → observe → feedback → re-teach).

### 9.5 Microteaching for novices (fast skill growth)
Microteaching studies describe short, focused teaching rehearsals with immediate feedback, reflection, and re-teach cycles; research reports improvements in teaching skills and confidence [[80]](https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1214270.pdf), and a review reports microteaching enhances competencies such as classroom management, instructional delivery, and reflective practice (with caveats about research quality variability) [[81]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1562975/full).  

**Use in your school:** novices practice one skill (e.g., “giving rationales + bounded choice” or “dialogic talk moves”) in a 7-minute micro-lesson, then redo it.

---

## 10) 2026 Implementation Conditions, Risks, and Future Directions (AI + Digital Reality)

Holistic empowerment now must include **digital and AI literacies**—but with child safety, equity, and privacy built in.

### 10.1 Children’s digital exposure is early and widespread
OECD reports that children’s digital engagement starts early (e.g., high internet access by age ~10; many already own smartphones) and highlights risks including problematic use, sleep/mental health impacts for vulnerable students, and calls for multi-sector approaches [[82]](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/2025/05/how-s-life-for-children-in-the-digital-age_c4a22655.html).  

**Implication:** elementary English tasks that use devices must be paired with digital wellbeing routines (time boundaries, active vs passive use, reflective discussions).

### 10.2 Online safety: move beyond “stranger danger” to design literacy
OECD describes online risk categories (content, conduct, contact, consumer) and stresses no single action solves all risks; digital literacy can be empowering [[83]](https://www.oecd.org/en/topics/sub-issues/children-in-the-digital-environment.html). The EU’s DSA minors’ guidelines recommend platform measures such as private-by-default accounts for minors, reducing recommender “rabbit holes,” disabling persuasive design features (autoplay, streaks, push notifications) by default, and strengthening blocking/reporting tools [[88]](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/library/commission-publishes-guidelines-protection-minors).  

**Classroom translation:** Teach children to recognize:
- autoplay loops,
- streak pressure,
- “recommended for you” manipulation,
- persuasive notifications,  
as part of media literacy.

### 10.3 Data privacy: generative AI makes “prompt hygiene” non-negotiable
England’s DfE guidance (2025) warns schools to avoid entering identifiable information into open generative AI tools; it distinguishes open vs closed tools and requires privacy notice transparency if personal data is used, noting tools may collect metadata (IP, location) and may share/sell data [[91]](https://www.gov.uk/guidance/data-protection-in-schools/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-and-data-protection-in-schools). DfE also stresses pupil use requires safeguards, supervision, and compliance with age restrictions [[90]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education).  

**Minimum school rule for elementary:** students never paste personal data into AI tools; teachers never paste identifiable student records into open tools.

### 10.4 AI governance and human override
Ofsted’s 2024 AI position says inspection will consider AI through existing criteria (safeguarding, quality) and expects leaders to ensure safety, data protection, bias monitoring, transparency, and that staff can overrule AI outputs [[94]](https://dera.ioe.ac.uk/id/eprint/40682/1/Ofsted%27s%20approach%20to%20artificial%20intelligence%20%28AI%29%20-%20GOV.UK.pdf). The U.S. Department of Education (OET) toolkit (2024) similarly frames safe, ethical, equitable AI integration through privacy/security, civil rights/accessibility, bias discrimination mitigation, and policy updates [[95]](https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/ED661924.pdf). UNESCO emphasizes inclusion and equity amid AI’s rapid change [[96]](https://www.unesco.org/en/digital-education/artificial-intelligence), and UNICEF’s 2025 child-centered AI guidance lists requirements including privacy, safety, fairness, transparency, inclusion, and skilling children for AI realities (and flags emerging risks such as AI companions and synthetic media harms) [[85]](https://www.unicef.org/innocenti/reports/policy-guidance-ai-children).  

**Implication for elementary English:** AI can support drafting practice or speaking rehearsal, but:
- teacher remains final evaluator,
- students must learn “AI can be wrong,”
- policies must protect privacy and equity.

### 10.5 Technology equity: avoid widening gaps
UNESCO’s GEM report warns about unequal connectivity, mixed evidence of impact, and risk that technology investment can divert resources from basics; globally, a minority of primary schools are connected to the internet, and home device/connectivity gaps compound inequality [[100]](https://gem-report-2023.unesco.org/technology-in-education/), [[101]](https://gem-report-2023.unesco.org/).  

**Practical policy:** every tech-based English task should have a **no-tech equivalent** (paper storyboards, oral recordings with teacher device, offline picture cards).

### 10.6 Media and Information Literacy (MIL) as core English curriculum
UNESCO’s MIL Curriculum e-version includes modules on privacy/data protection, misinformation/disinformation, and AI/social media competencies [[99]](https://www.unesco.org/mil4teachers/en/curriculum).  

**English-class integration idea:** “reading comprehension” becomes “source comprehension”: Who made this? Why? What is missing? What data does this app collect? (age-appropriate versions)

---

## 11) Suggested Keywords (for your paper + future resource searches)

Use these as database/search keywords and as “paper keywords”:

**Core paradigm keywords**
- holistic empowerment; whole-child education; WSCC model [[1]](https://www.ascd.org/whole-child), [[2]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_cdc_gov/healthyschools/wscc/index.htm)  
- learner agency; student voice; autonomy-supportive teaching; self-determination theory [[63]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2006_ReeveJang_JEP.pdf), [[6]](https://stial.ie/resources/Ryan%20and%20Deci%202020%20self%20determination%20theory.pdf)  
- UDL 3.0; universal design for learning; equity lens; identity and belonging [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/)  
- SEL; SAFE SEL; CASEL framework; restorative practices [[3]](https://casel.org/fundamentals-of-sel/what-is-the-casel-framework/), [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/)  
- culturally sustaining pedagogy; funds of knowledge; identity-affirming pedagogy [[14]](https://ies.ed.gov/rel-pacific/2025/01/culturally-sustaining-pedagogy-infographic), [[15]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/oa-edit/10.4324/9780203774977-18/culturally-sustaining-pedagogy-samy-alim-django-paris-casey-philip-wong)  

**Elementary English pedagogy keywords**
- task-based language teaching (young learners); input-based tasks [[62]](https://www.jbe-platform.com/content/journals/10.1075/ltyl.19005.ell)  
- guided play; playful learning; language games [[52]](https://ies.ed.gov/learn/blog/prioritizing-play-importance-play-based-learning-early-education), [[53]](https://www.naeyc.org/resources/pubs/yc/summer2022/power-playful-learning)  
- dialogic teaching; classroom talk moves [[51]](https://robinalexander.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/RPIE-2018-Alexander-dialogic-teaching.pdf)  
- cooperative learning; positive interdependence; group processing [[50]](http://www.co-operation.org/what-is-cooperative-learning)  
- sentence frames; academic language scaffolds [[59]](https://www.colorincolorado.org/teaching-ells/ell-classroom-strategy-library/sentence-frames)  
- SIOP model; sheltered instruction; language objectives [[46]](https://sites.google.com/erlc.ca/ell/archived-pages/programming/sheltered-instruction), [[71]](https://static.sched.com/hosted_files/siopnationalconference2019/73/8%20components%20with%20techniques.pdf)  
- translanguaging pedagogy; multilingual resources; bilingual scaffolding [[33]](https://tesl-ej.org/wordpress/issues/volume26/ej103/ej103a4/), [[48]](https://wida.wisc.edu/news/guide-translanguaging-classroom), [[111]](https://wida.wisc.edu/sites/default/files/resource/WIDA-ELD-Standards-Framework-2020-Edition-Grades-6-8.pdf)  
- multiliteracies; multimodal composition; digital storytelling [[26]](https://journals.library.ualberta.ca/langandlit/index.php/langandlit/article/view/29333)  

**Assessment keywords**
- formative assessment; peer feedback; self-assessment; portfolios [[117]](https://www.teachingenglish.org.uk/professional-development/teachers/teaching-knowledge-database/d-h/formative-assessment), [[124]](https://rm.coe.int/16804932bd)  
- WIDA Can Do Descriptors; ACCESS score interpretation; accommodations [[110]](https://wida.wisc.edu/teach/can-do/descriptors), [[112]](https://wida.wisc.edu/assess/access/scores-reports), [[113]](https://wida.wisc.edu/assess/accessibility-accommodations)  
- CEFR Companion Volume; mediation descriptors; plurilingual competence [[123]](https://rm.coe.int/common-european-framework-of-reference-for-languages-learning-teaching/16809ea0d4)  
- fairness in testing; accessibility; testing standards [[125]](https://ncme.org/resources/books/testing-standards/)  

**2026 digital/AI keywords**
- AI literacy; child-centered AI; privacy-by-design; prompt hygiene [[85]](https://www.unicef.org/innocenti/reports/policy-guidance-ai-children), [[91]](https://www.gov.uk/guidance/data-protection-in-schools/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-and-data-protection-in-schools)  
- online safety; persuasive design; recommender systems; DSA minors guidelines [[88]](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/library/commission-publishes-guidelines-protection-minors)  
- media and information literacy; misinformation; data protection [[99]](https://www.unesco.org/mil4teachers/en/curriculum)  
- digital wellbeing; problematic digital media use; screen time [[82]](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/2025/05/how-s-life-for-children-in-the-digital-age_c4a22655.html)

## 12) Practical Appendices (Include in your paper for novice teachers)

### Appendix A — “Holistic Empowerment” Classroom Look-Fors (Observation/Coaching Checklist)
**Agency & motivation**
- Bounded choices offered daily (topic/partner/product) [[63]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2006_ReeveJang_JEP.pdf), [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/)  
- Teacher provides rationales; uses informational feedback [[63]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2006_ReeveJang_JEP.pdf)  
- Students reflect with “I can / Next time I will…” (self-assessment routines) [[124]](https://rm.coe.int/16804932bd)

**Belonging & safety**
- Predictable routines explicitly taught and practiced [[45]](https://iris.peabody.vanderbilt.edu/module/beh2_elem/cresource/q2/p05/)  
- Calm space / re-entry routine exists (trauma-informed) [[64]](https://wasa-oly.org/WASA/images/WASA/6.0%20Resources/Hanover/BEST%20PRACTICES%20FOR%20TRAUMA-INFORMED%20INSTRUCTION%20.pdf), [[66]](https://www.nctsn.org/sites/default/files/resources/brief/supporting-trauma-informed-schools-to-keep-students-in-the-classroom-brief-1.pdf)  
- Errors treated as normal; no public shaming (supportive climate) [[3]](https://casel.org/fundamentals-of-sel/what-is-the-casel-framework/)

**Language growth**
- High opportunities to respond (choral, pair rehearsal) [[72]](https://dropoutprevention.org/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/Simonsen_Fairbanks_Briesch_Myers_Sugai_2008.pdf)  
- Sentence frames differentiated by proficiency and gradually faded [[59]](https://www.colorincolorado.org/teaching-ells/ell-classroom-strategy-library/sentence-frames)  
- Multimodal input/output (visuals, gestures, oral + written) [[111]](https://wida.wisc.edu/sites/default/files/resource/WIDA-ELD-Standards-Framework-2020-Edition-Grades-6-8.pdf), [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/)

**Equity & identity**
- Classroom texts/resources provide mirrors and windows; accessibility formats considered [[68]](https://nyslibrary.libguides.com/dei/mirrors)  
- Respect across languages/dialects is explicit (UDL 3.0) [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/)  
- Translanguaging used strategically for access + identity (not random) [[48]](https://wida.wisc.edu/news/guide-translanguaging-classroom), [[33]](https://tesl-ej.org/wordpress/issues/volume26/ej103/ej103a4/)

**Assessment**
- Formative checks daily guide next steps [[117]](https://www.teachingenglish.org.uk/professional-development/teachers/teaching-knowledge-database/d-h/formative-assessment)  
- Multiple evidence sources (work samples, speaking recordings, checklists)  
- Accommodations align with guidance; no invalid modifications [[113]](https://wida.wisc.edu/assess/accessibility-accommodations)

---

### Appendix B — Sample 5-Day Micro-Unit (Grades 3–5): “Our Community Helpers”
**Goal:** vocabulary + speaking confidence + belonging + agency

**Day 1 (Human + Engage):** community helper picture walk; students choose 2 helpers they admire (choice/autonomy) [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/), [[63]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2006_ReeveJang_JEP.pdf)  
**Day 2 (Access):** sentence frames: “A ___ helps us by ___.” (differentiated frames) [[59]](https://www.colorincolorado.org/teaching-ells/ell-classroom-strategy-library/sentence-frames)  
**Day 3 (Rehearse):** cooperative interviews with roles (speaker/encourager/reporter) [[50]](http://www.co-operation.org/what-is-cooperative-learning)  
**Day 4 (Transfer):** dialogic circle: “Which helper is most important and why?” with talk moves [[51]](https://robinalexander.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/RPIE-2018-Alexander-dialogic-teaching.pdf)  
**Day 5 (Show + Reflect):** multimodal product: mini-poster OR short audio speech (multiple expression modes) [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/); reflection: “I can explain… I will try…” [[124]](https://rm.coe.int/16804932bd)

**Assessment evidence:** teacher observation checklist + student audio sample + self-assessment checklist + peer feedback note [[117]](https://www.teachingenglish.org.uk/professional-development/teachers/teaching-knowledge-database/d-h/formative-assessment), [[124]](https://rm.coe.int/16804932bd).

---

## Conclusion (position statement you can use)
A holistic empowerment paradigm in elementary English is not a motivational slogan; it is a **designable, observable pedagogy** grounded in whole-child education [[1]](https://www.ascd.org/whole-child), WSCC collaboration [[2]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_cdc_gov/healthyschools/wscc/index.htm), SEL learning environments [[3]](https://casel.org/fundamentals-of-sel/what-is-the-casel-framework/), SDT motivation mechanisms with language-learning evidence [[7]](https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/2025_AlamerRobatEtAl_L2.pdf), UDL 3.0’s agency-and-equity design [[22]](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/), culturally sustaining identity commitments [[14]](https://ies.ed.gov/rel-pacific/2025/01/culturally-sustaining-pedagogy-infographic), [[15]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/oa-edit/10.4324/9780203774977-18/culturally-sustaining-pedagogy-samy-alim-django-paris-casey-philip-wong), and multilingual/translanguaging practices with documented benefits and constraints [[33]](https://tesl-ej.org/wordpress/issues/volume26/ej103/ej103a4/). For novice teachers, the paradigm becomes practical through routines, structured talk, guided play, cooperative roles, differentiated scaffolds, and formative assessment systems that document growth without narrowing learning [[45]](https://iris.peabody.vanderbilt.edu/module/beh2_elem/cresource/q2/p05/), [[52]](https://ies.ed.gov/learn/blog/prioritizing-play-importance-play-based-learning-early-education), [[50]](http://www.co-operation.org/what-is-cooperative-learning), [[59]](https://www.colorincolorado.org/teaching-ells/ell-classroom-strategy-library/sentence-frames), [[117]](https://www.teachingenglish.org.uk/professional-development/teachers/teaching-knowledge-database/d-h/formative-assessment). In 2026, empowering English education must also include AI/media literacy, privacy protection, online safety, and equity planning—so that innovation strengthens, rather than fractures, children’s belonging, agency, and opportunity [[90]](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/generative-artificial-intelligence-in-education/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-education), [[91]](https://www.gov.uk/guidance/data-protection-in-schools/generative-artificial-intelligence-ai-and-data-protection-in-schools), [[88]](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/library/commission-publishes-guidelines-protection-minors), [[100]](https://gem-report-2023.unesco.org/technology-in-education/), [[85]](https://www.unicef.org/innocenti/reports/policy-guidance-ai-children), [[99]](https://www.unesco.org/mil4teachers/en/curriculum).
